1:
"It is not expedient for me doubtless to glory. I will come to visions and revelations of the Lord." The brief verse notes Paul's reluctant turn to visions and revelations. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul says "it is not expedient (profitable) for me to glory" — boasting does him no good and is distasteful — yet, compelled by his opponents, he will go on to "visions and revelations of the Lord."

On "visions and revelations of the Lord," the witnesses note the new ground. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul now turns from boasting of his sufferings (ch. 11) to the extraordinary "visions and revelations" granted him by the Lord — manifestations of divine things, the highest of his credentials.

The Reformed Calvin notes the reluctance: Paul enters on this subject unwillingly, only because forced; he would rather be silent about such glories, and speaks as one constrained. The verse displays Paul's reluctant turn to visions and revelations.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul says "it is not expedient to glory," boasting being distasteful and unprofitable, yet, compelled, he will go on to "visions and revelations of the Lord" (Barnes, Robertson); turning from his sufferings to the extraordinary visions granted him, the highest of his credentials (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes he enters the subject unwillingly, only because forced. The verse displays the turn to visions. The verse marks the disclaimer: not expedient to glory. The verse displays the subject: visions and revelations. The verse displays the source: of the Lord. The verse displays the reluctance: constrained. The turn displays Paul reluctantly recounting his revelations. The verse displays the glorying: unprofitable. The verse displays the visions: of the Lord. The verse displays the constraint: upon him. Paul's reluctant turn to visions and revelations displays his distaste for boasting even as he is forced to it: though glorying profits him nothing, his opponents compel him to speak of the extraordinary visions and revelations the Lord granted him. The verse displays the apostle's constrained boast, opening on the vision of paradise (vv.2-4).
2:
"I knew a man in Christ above fourteen years ago, (whether in the body, I cannot tell; or whether out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth;) such an one caught up to the third heaven." The witnesses note the man caught up to the third heaven — Paul himself, in or out of the body. On "I knew a man in Christ," the grammatical voices note the modest concealment. Barnes and Robertson note Paul speaks "of a man in Christ" (a Christian) in the third person, but means himself — out of modesty and to avoid the appearance of boasting, he veils his own identity (confirmed by v.7).

On "whether in the body... or out of the body, I cannot tell," the witnesses note the mystery. Barnes and Calvin note Paul confesses he does not know whether his body was caught up or only his soul, in a state of trance; he leaves the manner to God, content with the fact and not pressing the mode.

On "caught up to the third heaven," the witnesses note the height. Barnes and Cyril note that "the third heaven" is the highest heaven, the dwelling-place of God (above the aerial and the starry heavens) — not the seven heavens of Jewish fable but the topmost, the abode of God and the blessed. The verse displays the man caught up to the third heaven — Paul himself.

The witnesses agree: Paul speaks "of a man in Christ" in the third person but means himself, veiling his identity out of modesty (confirmed by v.7, Barnes, Robertson); he confesses he does not know "whether in the body or out of the body" he was caught up, leaving the manner to God, content with the fact (Barnes, Calvin); and "the third heaven" is the highest heaven, the dwelling-place of God, not the seven heavens of fable (Barnes, Cyril). The verse displays the rapture to the third heaven. The verse marks the man: Paul himself. The verse displays the mystery: in or out of the body. The verse displays the height: the third heaven. The verse displays the modesty: veiled identity. The rapture displays Paul caught up to the highest heaven. The verse displays the man: in Christ. The verse displays the body: uncertain. The verse displays the heaven: the third. The man caught up to the third heaven — Paul himself, in or out of the body — displays the extraordinary revelation he reluctantly recounts: out of modesty he speaks as of another, confessing he knows not whether body or soul alone was caught up, but only that he was taken to the highest heaven, the dwelling-place of God. The verse displays the apostle's veiled rapture, continued into paradise (vv.3-4).
3:
"And I knew such a man, (whether in the body, or out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth;)" The brief verse notes the solemn repetition of the rapture and its mystery. The grammatical voices note the emphasis. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats "I knew such a man" and again the disclaimer "whether in the body or out of the body, I cannot tell" — a solemn repetition that emphasizes both the reality of the event and the mystery of its manner.

On "God knoweth," the witnesses note the deference. Barnes and Ellicott note Paul refers the unknown manner wholly to God: "God knoweth" — content to leave with God what he himself cannot explain; the fact is sure, the mode beyond him.

The grammatical-historical note the repetition's force: by repeating the words, Paul underscores that he makes no pretence of understanding the rapture; he affirms only what he knows and humbly leaves the rest. The verse displays the solemn repetition of the rapture and its mystery.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats "I knew such a man" and again the disclaimer "whether in the body or out of the body, I cannot tell," a solemn repetition emphasizing both the reality and the mystery (Barnes, Robertson); referring the unknown manner wholly to God, "God knoweth," content to leave with him what he cannot explain (Barnes, Ellicott). The grammatical-historical note Paul makes no pretence of understanding the rapture. The verse displays the repetition. The verse marks the repetition: I knew such a man. The verse displays the disclaimer: in or out of the body. The verse displays the deference: God knoweth. The verse displays the force: reality and mystery. The repetition displays Paul affirming the fact and leaving the manner to God. The verse displays the knowing: of the man. The verse displays the mystery: of the manner. The verse displays the God: who knows. The solemn repetition of the rapture and its mystery displays Paul's humble certainty: by repeating that he knew such a man yet cannot tell whether in or out of the body, he underscores both the reality of the event and his refusal to pretend to explain its manner, which he leaves with God. The verse displays the apostle's reverent reticence, completed in the vision of paradise (v.4).
4:
"How that he was caught up into paradise, and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter." The witnesses note the rapture into paradise and the unutterable words — with the question of one rapture or two. On "caught up into paradise," interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson take "paradise" as the same place as the third heaven (v.2) — the abode of God and the blessed, named differently. But Gill, with several ancients (Clement of Alexandria, Theophylact, Oecumenius) and Ellicott, takes it as a distinct stage or place — a second rapture, from the third heaven on into paradise (the place of the righteous souls' rest).

On "heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter," the witnesses note the silence. Barnes and Calvin note Paul heard "unspeakable (ineffable) words" which "it is not lawful (or possible) for a man to utter" — either things human language cannot express, or things he was not permitted to reveal; he keeps a reverent silence.

The Reformed Calvin notes the purpose: these revelations were given for Paul's own strengthening, to sustain him in his arduous trials, not for publication; and his silence rebukes vain curiosity about heavenly secrets. The verse displays the rapture into paradise and the unutterable words.

The witnesses agree: Paul "heard unspeakable (ineffable) words which it is not lawful (or possible) for a man to utter" — either beyond human language or not permitted to reveal — and keeps a reverent silence, the revelation given for his own strengthening, not publication (Barnes, Calvin). But "paradise" divides them: Barnes and Robertson take it as the same place as the third heaven, named differently; Gill, with the ancients (Clement, Theophylact, Oecumenius) and Ellicott, takes it as a distinct stage — a second rapture into the place of the righteous souls' rest. The verse displays the rapture into paradise. The verse marks the paradise: same or distinct. The verse displays the words: unutterable. The verse displays the silence: reverent. The verse displays the purpose: Paul's strengthening. The rapture displays Paul hearing what may not be told. The verse displays the paradise: entered. The verse displays the words: ineffable. The verse displays the silence: kept. The rapture into paradise and the unutterable words display the height and reticence of Paul's revelation: caught up into paradise — read as the third heaven itself or a further stage — he heard ineffable words he was not permitted to utter, keeping a reverent silence that rebukes vain curiosity, the vision given to strengthen him for his trials. The verse displays the apostle's holy reserve, in which he will glory only of his infirmities (v.5).
5:
"Of such an one will I glory: yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities." The brief verse notes Paul glorying in the favoured man yet only in his own infirmities. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's careful turn: "of such an one (the man caught up, himself viewed as the recipient of revelation) will I glory" — he may boast of that — "yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities."

On "in mine infirmities," the witnesses note the paradox. Barnes and Calvin note that, of himself personally, Paul will boast only of his "infirmities (weaknesses, sufferings)" — not of his revelations as if they were his own achievement; he glories in what shows his weakness, not his strength.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the humility: Paul keeps the glory of the revelation distinct from himself, lest he seem to claim merit; his only personal boast is in the weaknesses that magnify God's grace. The verse displays Paul glorying in the favoured man yet only in his own infirmities.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's careful turn — "of such an one will I glory" (he may boast of the man caught up) "yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities" (Barnes, Robertson); of himself personally he boasts only of his weaknesses and sufferings, not of his revelations as an achievement (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes he keeps the revelation's glory distinct from himself, lest he claim merit. The verse displays glorying in infirmities. The verse marks the distinction: the man vs himself. The verse displays the boast: in infirmities. The verse displays the humility: not in achievement. The verse displays the paradox: glory in weakness. The distinction displays Paul boasting only in what shows his weakness. The verse displays the glorying: of the man. The verse displays the infirmities: his only boast. The verse displays the humility: maintained. Paul glorying in the favoured man yet only in his own infirmities displays his careful humility: he may boast of the man caught up to heaven, but of himself he will glory only in his weaknesses — not in the revelations as his own, but in what magnifies God's grace and his own dependence. The verse displays the apostle's boast in weakness, restrained from undue self-praise (v.6).
6:
"For though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool; for I will say the truth: but now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me." The brief verse notes Paul forbearing to boast lest he be overrated. The grammatical voices note the restraint. Barnes and Robertson note Paul says that even "if he would desire to glory, he shall not be a fool" — his boast would be no empty folly, "for I will say the truth"; his claims are all true and could be justly made.

On "now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth," the witnesses note the reason. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul nonetheless "forbears (refrains)" — he holds back from boasting — "lest any man should think of him above" what they actually see in him or hear from him; he will not be rated higher than his conduct and teaching warrant.

The Reformed Calvin notes the modesty: Paul would have men judge him by what is plainly seen in his life and ministry, not by extraordinary revelations; he refuses to trade on his visions for reputation. The verse displays Paul forbearing to boast lest he be overrated.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: even "if Paul would glory, he shall not be a fool, for I will say the truth," his claims all true and justly made (Barnes, Robertson); yet he "forbears," holding back "lest any man should think of him above" what they see or hear, refusing to be rated higher than his conduct warrants (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes he would be judged by his plain life, not his visions. The verse displays Paul forbearing to boast. The verse marks the truth: no empty folly. The verse displays the forbearance: he refrains. The verse displays the reason: not overrated. The verse displays the modesty: judged by his life. The forbearance displays Paul refusing to trade on his visions. The verse displays the glorying: justified but withheld. The verse displays the truth: of his claims. The verse displays the restraint: maintained. Paul forbearing to boast lest he be overrated displays his sober modesty: though his boast would be no folly, since it is all true, he refrains from it — unwilling to be esteemed higher than his visible life and teaching warrant, refusing to trade on his revelations for reputation. The verse displays the apostle's restraint, which God himself secured by the thorn (v.7).
7:
"And lest I should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I should be exalted above measure." The witnesses note the thorn in the flesh given to keep Paul humble — the great crux of its nature. On "lest I should be exalted above measure," the grammatical voices note the purpose. Barnes and Robertson note God's design: to keep Paul from spiritual pride after such revelations, "a thorn in the flesh" was "given" him (the word implying a gift, a favour) — repeated for emphasis: lest he be exalted overmuch.

On the nature of the "thorn," interpreters sharply divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott take it as some chronic bodily ailment (eye-disease, neuralgia/headache, epilepsy, or the like) — a physical malady; Barnes wisely judges all confident identification mere conjecture. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke (with Chrysostom) takes it as the persecution and distress from his opponents, especially the false apostle. The Reformed Calvin takes it as every kind of temptation against the unregenerate flesh — and, with Barnes, sharply rejects as "ridiculous" the view (held by many Latin and Romish Fathers, and entertained by Ellicott as a supplement) that it was sensual lust.

On "the messenger of Satan to buffet me," the witnesses note the agent. Barnes and Calvin note it was a "messenger (angel) of Satan" — Satan permitted to afflict him (as with Job) — yet overruled by God for good, to "buffet (strike, humble)" him. The verse displays the thorn given to keep Paul humble.

The witnesses agree the purpose and agent: to keep Paul from pride after his revelations, a "thorn in the flesh" was given him — "the messenger of Satan to buffet him," Satan permitted (as with Job) yet overruled by God for good (Barnes, Calvin). But its nature is the great crux: Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott take it as a chronic bodily ailment (Barnes judging all identification conjecture); Clarke and Chrysostom take it as persecution from opponents and the false apostle; Calvin takes it as every kind of temptation against the flesh; and the sensual-lust reading of the Latin/Romish Fathers (entertained by Ellicott) is sharply rejected by Calvin and Barnes as "ridiculous." The verse displays the thorn in the flesh. The verse marks the purpose: lest exalted. The verse displays the debate: ailment, persecution, or temptation. The verse displays the agent: the messenger of Satan. The verse displays the overruling: God for good. The thorn displays Paul's affliction keeping him humble. The verse displays the thorn: in the flesh. The verse displays the buffeting: of Satan. The verse displays the humbling: intended. The thorn in the flesh given to keep Paul humble displays both God's gracious discipline and the famous crux: lest the abundance of revelations exalt him, a painful affliction — Satan's buffeting messenger overruled by God — was given him; whether a bodily ailment (Barnes, Ellicott), the persecution of foes (Clarke, Chrysostom), or temptation of the flesh (Calvin, rejecting the lust-reading) remains unresolved by Paul's own reticence. The verse displays the humbling thorn, met by thrice-repeated prayer (v.8).
8:
"For this thing I besought the Lord thrice, that it might depart from me." The brief verse notes Paul's threefold prayer for the thorn's removal. The grammatical voices note the earnest petition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "besought the Lord thrice" — prayed earnestly and repeatedly (as Christ prayed thrice in Gethsemane) — "that it (the thorn) might depart from me"; he sought relief from the painful affliction.

On "the Lord," the witnesses note the object of prayer. Barnes and Calvin note "the Lord" here is Christ (as v.9 shows, "the power of Christ") — Paul prayed directly to Christ, an incidental but real testimony to Christ's deity and his right to be invoked.

The puritan Henry notes the lesson: troubles drive us to prayer, and if relief is not granted at once, we are to persevere; Paul's thrice-repeated prayer teaches importunity. The verse displays Paul's threefold prayer for the thorn's removal.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "besought the Lord thrice" — earnestly and repeatedly (as Christ in Gethsemane) — "that it might depart from me," seeking relief from the affliction (Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord" being Christ (as v.9 shows), Paul praying directly to him, a testimony to his deity (Barnes, Calvin). Henry notes troubles drive us to prayer and teach perseverance. The verse displays the threefold prayer. The verse marks the prayer: thrice. The verse displays the petition: that it depart. The verse displays the object: the Lord Christ. The verse displays the lesson: importunity. The prayer displays Paul earnestly seeking the thorn's removal. The verse displays the beseeching: thrice. The verse displays the Lord: Christ. The verse displays the perseverance: in prayer. Paul's threefold prayer for the thorn's removal displays his earnest importunity: he besought the Lord — Christ himself — three times that the painful affliction might depart, teaching that troubles drive us to prayer and that we are to persevere even when relief is not at once granted. The verse displays the apostle's repeated petition, answered not by removal but by sufficient grace (v.9). That the apostle, who could work miracles of healing upon others, should himself be left with an unhealed affliction after such earnest and repeated prayer, is itself a lesson, teaching that the highest saint is not exempt from suffering, and that God answers the prayer of faith not always by granting the thing asked, but sometimes by giving a grace that turns the very trial into a means of blessing.
9:
"And he said unto me, My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness. Most gladly therefore will I rather glory in my infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest upon me." The witnesses note the answer: sufficient grace and strength perfected in weakness. On "my grace is sufficient for thee," the grammatical voices note the answer. Barnes and Robertson note Christ answered Paul's prayer not by removing the thorn but with a better gift: "my grace is sufficient for thee" — the support of his grace would be enough to bear it; the affliction would remain, but with grace to endure.

On "my strength is made perfect in weakness," the witnesses note the principle. Barnes and Calvin note the great paradox: "my strength is made perfect (displayed in its fulness) in weakness" — Christ's power shines most clearly when human strength fails; weakness is the very occasion for divine strength.

On "rather glory in my infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest upon me," the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the response: so Paul will "most gladly" glory in his weaknesses, that "the power of Christ may rest (tabernacle, dwell) upon him" — he welcomes weakness as the dwelling-place of Christ's power. The verse displays the answer: sufficient grace and strength perfected in weakness.

The witnesses agree: Christ answered not by removing the thorn but with a better gift — "my grace is sufficient for thee," enough to bear it (Barnes, Robertson); "my strength is made perfect (displayed in fulness) in weakness," Christ's power shining most when human strength fails (Barnes, Calvin); so Paul will "most gladly glory in his infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest (tabernacle) upon him," welcoming weakness as the home of Christ's power (Henry, Wesley). The verse displays sufficient grace. The verse marks the answer: grace sufficient. The verse displays the paradox: strength in weakness. The verse displays the response: glory in infirmities. The verse displays the indwelling: Christ's power resting. The answer displays Christ's strength perfected in Paul's weakness. The verse displays the grace: sufficient. The verse displays the strength: in weakness. The verse displays the power: resting on him. The answer — sufficient grace and strength perfected in weakness — displays the great comfort of the afflicted: Christ did not remove the thorn but gave grace enough to bear it, his power being made perfect in weakness; so Paul gladly glories in his infirmities, that the power of Christ may dwell upon him. The verse displays the strength of Christ in weakness, embraced with pleasure in suffering (v.10).
10:
"Therefore I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake: for when I am weak, then am I strong." The witnesses note Paul's pleasure in weakness, for then he is strong. On "I take pleasure in infirmities... for Christ's sake," the grammatical voices note the embrace of suffering. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having learned the lesson, now "takes pleasure (is content, even glad)" in a whole train of trials — "infirmities, reproaches, necessities, persecutions, distresses" — endured "for Christ's sake."

On "when I am weak, then am I strong," the witnesses note the paradox. Barnes and Calvin note the summing truth: "when I am weak (in myself), then am I strong" (in Christ's power) — for his very weakness is the occasion of Christ's strength resting on him; conscious weakness is the path to divine strength.

The puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the triumph: this is the Christian paradox — the believer is never so strong as when, emptied of self-confidence, he leans wholly on Christ; Paul rejoices in what drives him to that dependence. The verse displays Paul's pleasure in weakness, for then he is strong.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul now "takes pleasure (is content, even glad)" in a train of trials — "infirmities, reproaches, necessities, persecutions, distresses" — endured "for Christ's sake" (Barnes, Robertson); the summing truth being "when I am weak (in myself), then am I strong (in Christ's power)," his weakness the occasion of Christ's strength (Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Wesley note the believer is strongest when, emptied of self, he leans wholly on Christ. The verse displays pleasure in weakness. The verse marks the pleasure: in trials. The verse displays the cause: for Christ's sake. The verse displays the paradox: weak then strong. The verse displays the dependence: wholly on Christ. The pleasure displays Paul strong in Christ when weak in himself. The verse displays the trials: embraced. The verse displays the weakness: his own. The verse displays the strength: Christ's. Paul's pleasure in weakness, for then he is strong, displays the Christian paradox at its height: having learned that Christ's power rests on the weak, he takes pleasure in every trial endured for Christ — for when he is weak in himself, then is he strong in the strength of Christ, on whom he wholly leans. The verse displays the triumph of strength-in-weakness, closing the boast he was forced to make (v.11).
11:
"I am become a fool in glorying; ye have compelled me: for I ought to have been commended of you: for in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles, though I be nothing." The witnesses note Paul forced into foolish boasting, equal to the chiefest apostles yet nothing. On "I am become a fool in glorying; ye have compelled me," the grammatical voices note the constraint. Barnes and Robertson note Paul says his boasting has made him "a fool," but "ye have compelled me" — the Corinthians, by listening to his detractors, forced him to it; the folly is laid at their door.

On "I ought to have been commended of you," the witnesses note the reproach. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul's gentle rebuke: the Corinthians themselves "ought to have commended" him (defended his character) instead of forcing him to commend himself; they failed in their duty to him.

On "in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles, though I be nothing," the Reformed Calvin notes the balance of dignity and humility: Paul is "in nothing behind the very chiefest (the most eminent) apostles" — equal to the greatest — yet "though I be nothing" in himself; high in office, lowly in self-estimate. The verse displays Paul forced into foolish boasting, equal to the chiefest apostles yet nothing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's boasting has made him "a fool," but "ye have compelled me," the Corinthians forcing him by listening to his detractors (Barnes, Robertson); they "ought to have commended" him themselves instead of forcing self-commendation, a gentle rebuke (Barnes, Chrysostom); he is "in nothing behind the very chiefest apostles" yet "nothing" in himself — high in office, lowly in self-estimate (Calvin). The verse displays the forced boast. The verse marks the folly: compelled. The verse displays the reproach: ye should have commended me. The verse displays the dignity: equal to the chiefest. The verse displays the humility: yet nothing. The boast displays Paul equal to the apostles yet nothing in himself. The verse displays the fool: he became. The verse displays the compulsion: by them. The verse displays the equality: with the chiefest. Paul forced into foolish boasting, equal to the chiefest apostles yet nothing, displays both his constraint and his balanced self-view: the Corinthians compelled him to commend himself, failing to defend him; he is behind the greatest apostles in nothing, yet in himself is nothing — high in calling, humble in heart. The verse displays the apostle's reluctant self-vindication, backed by the signs of his apostleship (v.12).
12:
"Truly the signs of an apostle were wrought among you in all patience, in signs, and wonders, and mighty deeds." The brief verse notes the signs of an apostle wrought among the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the proof. Barnes and Robertson note Paul appeals to "the signs of an apostle" that "were wrought among you" — the marks that authenticated a true apostle, performed in their very sight; his apostleship was proved among them.

On "in signs, and wonders, and mighty deeds," the witnesses note the miracles. Barnes and Calvin note the threefold description (as elsewhere in Scripture): "signs" (significant tokens), "wonders" (marvels exciting awe), and "mighty deeds" (works of power, miracles) — the supernatural credentials of his office.

On "in all patience," the witnesses note the manner. Barnes notes Paul wrought these "in all patience (endurance)" — amid much suffering and opposition; his patient endurance was itself a mark of his apostleship, alongside the miracles. The verse displays the signs of an apostle wrought among the Corinthians.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul appeals to "the signs of an apostle" wrought "among you," the authenticating marks performed in their sight (Barnes, Robertson); the threefold "signs, wonders, and mighty deeds" — the supernatural credentials of his office (Barnes, Calvin); and "in all patience (endurance)," his patient suffering itself a mark of apostleship (Barnes). The verse displays the signs of an apostle. The verse marks the proof: signs of an apostle. The verse displays the miracles: signs, wonders, deeds. The verse displays the manner: in all patience. The verse displays the authentication: among them. The signs display Paul's apostleship proved by miracle and endurance. The verse displays the signs: wrought. The verse displays the wonders: performed. The verse displays the patience: shown. The signs of an apostle wrought among the Corinthians display the proof of Paul's office: the authenticating marks — signs, wonders, and mighty deeds, performed in their very sight and with patient endurance amid opposition — established his apostleship beyond question among them. The verse displays the credentials of the true apostle, who took nothing from them (v.13).
13:
"For what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you? forgive me this wrong." The brief verse notes Paul's irony that his only "wrong" was not burdening them. The grammatical voices note the comparison. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks "what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches" — the Corinthians lacked nothing other churches had — "except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you" (did not take support from them).

On "forgive me this wrong," the witnesses note the irony. Barnes and Calvin note the biting irony: the only "wrong" Paul did them was refusing to be a financial burden — he supported himself rather than take their money — and he asks, ironically, "forgive me this wrong."

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: Paul's enemies had twisted his refusal of support into a grievance (as if it proved he was no true apostle, or did not love them); he answers with pointed irony, exposing the absurdity. The verse displays Paul's irony that his only wrong was not burdening them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asks "what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches," the Corinthians lacking nothing others had, "except that I was not burdensome to you" (did not take support, Barnes, Robertson); the biting irony being that his only "wrong" was supporting himself rather than taking their money — "forgive me this wrong" (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes his enemies had twisted his refusal of support into a grievance. The verse displays the irony of the wrong. The verse marks the comparison: not inferior to other churches. The verse displays the exception: not burdensome. The verse displays the irony: forgive this wrong. The verse displays the twist: refusal made a grievance. The irony displays Paul's self-support absurdly counted a wrong. The verse displays the inferiority: none. The verse displays the burden: not laid. The verse displays the forgiveness: ironically asked. Paul's irony that his only "wrong" was not burdening them displays his pointed answer to a twisted charge: the Corinthians were inferior to no other church except that he refused to take their support — and for this one "wrong," supporting himself rather than burdening them, he ironically begs their forgiveness. The verse displays the apostle's biting wit, leading to his coming and his selfless love (vv.14-15).
14:
"Behold, the third time I am ready to come to you; and I will not be burdensome to you: for I seek not yours, but you: for the children ought not to lay up for the parents, but the parents for the children." The witnesses note Paul seeking the Corinthians themselves, not their goods, as a parent. On "the third time I am ready to come... I will not be burdensome," the grammatical voices note the resolve. Barnes and Robertson note Paul announces his readiness to come "the third time" and repeats that he "will not be burdensome" — he will again refuse their support, keeping his settled practice.

On "I seek not yours, but you," the witnesses note the motive. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's beautiful disavowal of self-interest: "I seek not yours (your money, your goods) but you (yourselves, your souls, your salvation)" — his desire is for them, not their possessions.

On "the parents for the children," the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the figure: as "parents lay up for the children," not children for parents, so Paul, their spiritual father, will provide for them rather than draw from them; his love is paternal. The verse displays Paul seeking the Corinthians themselves, not their goods.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul announces readiness to come "the third time" and repeats he "will not be burdensome," again refusing their support (Barnes, Robertson); his motive "I seek not yours, but you" — desiring themselves and their salvation, not their money (Barnes, Calvin); and as "parents lay up for children," so he, their spiritual father, provides for them rather than draws from them (Henry, Wesley). The verse displays seeking them, not theirs. The verse marks the coming: the third time. The verse displays the refusal: not burdensome. The verse displays the motive: not yours, but you. The verse displays the figure: parents for children. The motive displays Paul's paternal love seeking the Corinthians. The verse displays the readiness: to come. The verse displays the seeking: of them. The verse displays the parent: providing. Paul seeking the Corinthians themselves, not their goods, as a parent, displays the selflessness of his love: ready to come a third time, he will again take no support, for he seeks not their money but themselves — like a father laying up for his children, not drawing from them. The verse displays the apostle's paternal devotion, ready to spend himself for them (v.15).
15:
"And I will very gladly spend and be spent for you; though the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved." The brief verse notes Paul's gladness to spend and be spent, though loved the less. The grammatical voices note the self-sacrifice. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares he "will very gladly spend (lay out all he has) and be spent (be utterly used up, exhausted, even unto death) for you" — he will give not only his goods but himself for their good.

On "the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved," the witnesses note the painful return. Barnes and Calvin note the grief beneath the love: the more Paul loves them, the less he is loved in return — they requite his abundant love with coldness; yet this does not lessen his devotion.

The Reformed Calvin notes the model: true pastoral love loves on, even when unrequited; Paul's willingness to be spent for those who love him less is the mark of a genuine shepherd, imitating Christ. The verse displays Paul's gladness to spend and be spent, though loved the less.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "will very gladly spend (lay out all) and be spent (be utterly used up, even unto death) for you," giving not only his goods but himself (Barnes, Robertson); the grief beneath being that "the more he loves them, the less he is loved," his abundant love met with coldness, yet his devotion undiminished (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes true pastoral love loves on even when unrequited. The verse displays spending and being spent. The verse marks the sacrifice: spend and be spent. The verse displays the cost: even himself. The verse displays the grief: loved the less. The verse displays the model: love unrequited. The gladness displays Paul's love spending itself for the ungrateful. The verse displays the spending: of all. The verse displays the love: abundant. The verse displays the return: cold. Paul's gladness to spend and be spent, though loved the less, displays the depth of his pastoral love: he will gladly lay out all he has and be utterly used up for the Corinthians — and though his abundant love is met with coldness, he loves on undiminished, after the pattern of Christ. The verse displays the apostle's self-giving devotion, answering the slander of craftiness (vv.16-18).
16:
"But be it so, I did not burden you: nevertheless, being crafty, I caught you with guile." The brief verse notes Paul taking up the slander that he caught them by craft. The grammatical voices note the irony. Barnes and Robertson note Paul ironically states the slander of his opponents: granting that he "did not burden" them directly, they charged that "being crafty, I caught you with guile" — that he took their money indirectly, by cunning, through his agents.

On the charge, the witnesses note its substance. Barnes and Chrysostom note the insinuation: that Paul, while pretending not to take support, secretly enriched himself through the collection or through the brethren he sent — a slander on his integrity which he now refutes (vv.17-18).

The Reformed Calvin notes the bitterness: that even Paul's refusal of support was twisted into a proof of cunning shows the malice of his foes; he repeats their charge in order to expose and demolish it. The verse displays Paul taking up the slander that he caught them by craft.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul ironically states the slander — granting he "did not burden" them directly, his foes charged that "being crafty, I caught you with guile," taking their money indirectly through his agents (Barnes, Robertson); the insinuation that he secretly enriched himself through the collection, a slander on his integrity he will refute (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes the malice that twisted even his refusal of support into cunning. The verse displays the slander of craft. The verse marks the concession: did not burden. The verse displays the charge: caught with guile. The verse displays the insinuation: secret gain. The verse displays the malice: of his foes. The slander displays Paul's integrity attacked even in his selflessness. The verse displays the craft: alleged. The verse displays the guile: charged. The verse displays the refutation: to come. Paul taking up the slander that he caught them by craft displays the malice he must answer: his enemies, unable to fault his refusal of support, charged that he cunningly took their money through his agents — a slander on his integrity that he repeats only to expose and demolish. The verse displays the charge of guile, refuted by the conduct of his messengers (vv.17-18).
17:
"Did I make a gain of you by any of them whom I sent unto you?" The brief verse notes Paul's challenge that he gained nothing through his messengers. The grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul answers the slander with a direct challenge: "Did I make a gain of you (defraud you, profit at your expense) by any of them whom I sent unto you?" — let them name a single instance.

On the challenge, the witnesses note its force. Barnes and Wesley note the appeal to fact: Paul sent various messengers (Titus and others), and not one of them had ever taken advantage of the Corinthians; the charge of indirect gain collapses against the evidence.

The grammatical-historical note the confidence: Paul can appeal openly to their own knowledge, certain that no instance of fraud can be produced; his integrity and his agents' is unblemished. The verse displays Paul's challenge that he gained nothing through his messengers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul answers the slander with a direct challenge — "Did I make a gain of you (defraud you, profit at your expense) by any whom I sent?" — let them name an instance (Barnes, Robertson); the appeal to fact, for not one of his messengers had ever taken advantage of them, the charge collapsing against the evidence (Barnes, Wesley). The grammatical-historical note Paul appeals confidently to their own knowledge. The verse displays the challenge. The verse marks the challenge: did I gain by them? The verse displays the appeal: to fact. The verse displays the messengers: blameless. The verse displays the confidence: open appeal. The challenge displays Paul's integrity proved by his agents' conduct. The verse displays the gain: none. The verse displays the messengers: sent. The verse displays the fraud: disproved. Paul's challenge that he gained nothing through his messengers displays his open answer to the slander of guile: he appeals to the Corinthians' own knowledge — not one of the men he sent ever defrauded them — so the charge of indirect gain collapses against the plain evidence of his agents' integrity. The verse displays the apostle's confident appeal, confirmed in Titus's conduct (v.18).
18:
"I desired Titus, and with him I sent a brother. Did Titus make a gain of you? walked we not in the same spirit? walked we not in the same steps?" The brief verse notes the appeal to Titus's blameless conduct as proof. The grammatical voices note the instance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul names a specific case: "I desired (urged) Titus, and with him I sent a brother" — and asks, "Did Titus make a gain of you?" The answer is plainly no; Titus had taken nothing.

On "walked we not in the same spirit... the same steps?" the witnesses note the consistency. Barnes and JFB note Paul's point: he and Titus "walked in the same spirit (disposition) and the same steps (conduct)" — there was no difference between them; both acted with the same disinterested integrity.

The grammatical-historical note the proof: since Titus, Paul's representative, took nothing and acted exactly as Paul did, the charge that Paul cunningly profited through his agents is shown to be baseless. The verse displays the appeal to Titus's blameless conduct as proof.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul names a case — "I desired Titus, and with him I sent a brother" — and asks "Did Titus make a gain of you?" the answer plainly no (Barnes, Robertson); he and Titus "walked in the same spirit and the same steps," no difference between them, both acting with disinterested integrity (Barnes, JFB). The grammatical-historical note that since Titus took nothing, the charge is baseless. The verse displays Titus's blameless conduct. The verse marks the instance: Titus and the brother. The verse displays the question: did Titus gain? The verse displays the consistency: same spirit, same steps. The verse displays the proof: charge baseless. The appeal displays Titus's integrity proving Paul's. The verse displays the Titus: blameless. The verse displays the walking: in step. The verse displays the proof: established. The appeal to Titus's blameless conduct as proof displays the decisive answer to the slander: Titus, sent as Paul's representative, took nothing and walked in the very same spirit and steps as Paul — so the charge that Paul profited cunningly through his agents is shown to be groundless. The verse displays the integrity of Paul and his colleagues, all done for the Corinthians' edifying (v.19).
19:
"Again, think ye that we excuse ourselves unto you? we speak before God in Christ: but we do all things, dearly beloved, for your edifying." The brief verse notes Paul speaking before God, all for the Corinthians' edifying. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul asks "think ye that we excuse (defend) ourselves unto you?" — as if his whole concern were merely to clear his own name before them; he disclaims any such low motive.

On "we speak before God in Christ," the witnesses note the solemnity. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's solemn appeal: he speaks "before God in Christ" — in God's presence, with full sincerity, not for human approval but as accountable to God; his defense is no mere self-justification.

On "all things... for your edifying," the witnesses note the aim. Barnes and Wesley note the true purpose: Paul does "all things" — including this whole defense — "for your edifying (building up)," for the Corinthians' spiritual good, not his own vindication. The verse displays Paul speaking before God, all for their edifying.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul disclaims that his concern is merely to "excuse (defend) himself" before them (Barnes, Robertson); he speaks "before God in Christ," in God's presence with full sincerity, accountable to God not men (Barnes, Calvin); doing "all things for your edifying," for the Corinthians' good, not his own vindication (Barnes, Wesley). The verse displays speaking before God. The verse marks the disclaimer: not mere self-defense. The verse displays the solemnity: before God in Christ. The verse displays the aim: their edifying. The verse displays the love: dearly beloved. The disclaimer displays Paul's defense aimed at their building up. The verse displays the excusing: disclaimed. The verse displays the God: before whom he speaks. The verse displays the edifying: his aim. Paul speaking before God, all for the Corinthians' edifying, displays the true motive of his whole defense: he is not merely clearing his own name, but speaks sincerely in God's presence, and does all things — even this self-vindication — for the building up of those he loves. The verse displays the apostle's God-witnessed aim, voiced in fear for their state (vv.20-21).
20:
"For I fear, lest, when I come, I shall not find you such as I would, and that I shall be found unto you such as ye would not: lest there be debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults:" The brief verse notes Paul's fear of finding sins and disorders at Corinth. The grammatical voices note the apprehension. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "fears, lest when I come, I shall not find you such as I would" (not in the state he desires) "and that I shall be found unto you such as ye would not" — that he may have to come in severity, which they would not wish.

On the catalogue of sins, the witnesses note the disorders. Barnes and Calvin note the list of evils Paul fears still persist among them: "debates (strife), envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings (secret slanders), swellings (pride, conceit), tumults (disorders)" — the sins of a divided, contentious church.

The Reformed Calvin notes the pastoral grief: Paul names these sins not to insult them but in loving fear, that they may amend before he comes and spare him the painful necessity of discipline. The verse displays Paul's fear of finding sins and disorders at Corinth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "fears lest, when I come, I shall not find you such as I would, and shall be found such as ye would not" — that he may have to come in severity (Barnes, Robertson); the list of evils he fears persist — "debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults," the sins of a divided church (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes he names them in loving fear, that they may amend. The verse displays the feared disorders. The verse marks the fear: not as I would. The verse displays the catalogue: strifes and envyings. The verse displays the severity: such as ye would not. The verse displays the grief: pastoral. The fear displays Paul dreading the sins of a contentious church. The verse displays the finding: feared. The verse displays the sins: listed. The verse displays the warning: to amend. Paul's fear of finding sins and disorders at Corinth displays his pastoral anxiety: he dreads coming to find them unchanged — torn by strife, envy, wrath, slander, pride, and tumult — and being forced to deal sharply with them; he names these evils that they may amend before he arrives. The verse displays the apostle's loving apprehension, deepened by fear for the impenitent (v.21).
21:
"And lest, when I come again, my God will humble me among you, and that I shall bewail many which have sinned already, and have not repented of the uncleanness and fornication and lasciviousness which they have committed." The witnesses note Paul's fear of having to mourn the unrepentant impure. On "lest... my God will humble me among you," the grammatical voices note the dread. Barnes and Robertson note Paul fears that, on his return, "my God will humble me among you" — that he will be brought low, grieved and ashamed, by finding sin still among them; their sin would be his humiliation.

On "I shall bewail many which have sinned already, and have not repented," the witnesses note the grief. Barnes and Calvin note Paul fears he must "bewail (mourn over)" many who had sinned and "not repented" — those who had fallen into gross sins and remained impenitent; their hardness would cause him deep sorrow.

On "the uncleanness and fornication and lasciviousness," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the specific sins: the sins of impurity (uncleanness, fornication, lasciviousness) that had marked the corrupt Corinthian culture and still ensnared some in the church; Paul grieves that some had not forsaken them. The verse displays Paul's fear of having to mourn the unrepentant impure.

The witnesses agree: Paul fears that on his return "my God will humble me among you," brought low and grieved by finding sin still among them (Barnes, Robertson); that he must "bewail (mourn over) many which have sinned and not repented," fallen into gross sins and impenitent, their hardness causing him deep sorrow (Barnes, Calvin); the sins being those of impurity — "uncleanness, fornication, lasciviousness" — that still ensnared some (Wesley). The verse displays the fear of mourning the impenitent. The verse marks the dread: God humble me. The verse displays the grief: bewail many. The verse displays the impenitence: not repented. The verse displays the sins: impurity. The fear displays Paul grieved by the unrepentant impure. The verse displays the humbling: of Paul. The verse displays the mourning: over sinners. The verse displays the uncleanness: unrepented. Paul's fear of having to mourn the unrepentant impure displays the depth of his pastoral grief: he dreads that on his return he will be humbled and made to mourn over the many who had fallen into uncleanness, fornication, and lasciviousness and had not repented — their hardness his sorrow. The verse displays the apostle's grief over impenitent sin, closing the chapter and leading to his solemn warning of discipline (ch. 13).
